sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture and biosecurity agencies (DPIRD Western Australia, Business Queensland, the Queensland Department of Agriculture and Fisheries and NSW Local Land Services), national horticultural research, and recognised nursery and industry sources.

core.intro: Apricots are the first stone fruit of summer and one of the most rewarding to grow, but they are also the fussiest: they want a properly cold winter, dry spring weather, sharp drainage and a variety matched to your district, and they sulk where it is humid or waterlogged. Get the site and variety right and a single tree gives a heavy crop. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Apricots range from early, lower-chill types for milder districts to rich, heritage varieties that need a cold winter. Trevatt is an Australian heirloom and the classic drying and canning apricot, firm-fleshed and reliable, ripening early to mid season. Moorpark is the famous old all-rounder, a large, deeply flavoured apricot for eating, jam and bottling that ripens a little later and wants real winter cold. Storey's is a versatile Australian variety good for eating, bottling and drying, and Divinity is a long-established type with large, sweet fruit. For warmer gardens, Glengarry is an early, heavy-cropping yellow-fleshed apricot, Newcastle (also sold as Newcastle Early) is an early low-chill variety, and Fireball is a low-chill type often sold as a dwarf. Decide whether you want fruit mainly for eating fresh or for drying and bottling, then match the variety to your winter chill. You will also see plum and apricot crosses, such as the plumcot Plumscrumptious, sold alongside the apricots.

sections.heading: Matching the variety to your winter chill
sections.body: Apricots are deciduous and need a spell of winter cold to wake up and crop, measured as chill hours below about 7 degrees. They are not as forgiving as peaches: even the low-chill apricots (Newcastle, Glengarry and Fireball) still want roughly 300 to 400 hours and suit milder-winter districts, while the mid-season favourites such as Trevatt and Bulida want more, and the richly flavoured heritage types like Moorpark, Storey's and Divinity crop best where winters are properly cold. Plant a high-chill variety somewhere too mild and you get patchy budburst and a poor crop, so matching the variety to your district is the single most important choice you make. Apricots also flower earliest of all the stone fruit, which makes their blossom the most exposed to late frost in cold areas.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree is enough
sections.body: Almost all apricots are self-fertile, so a single tree sets a full crop with no second variety needed, which makes them an easy choice for a small garden and the main thing that sets them apart from most plums and cherries. There are a couple of exceptions worth knowing: the low-chill Fireball is only partially self-fertile and fruits better with a plumcot such as Spring Satin nearby, and Divinity, while self-fertile, bears more heavily with another apricot close by. For every other common variety, one tree on its own is fine.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give apricots full sun and deep, free-draining soil. They are very sensitive to wet feet and will not tolerate a spot where water pools, so plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy and never in a hollow. Apricots cope with a near-neutral to slightly alkaline soil better than most fruit trees (around pH 6.5 to 8), so a dressing of lime suits them where the ground is acidic. Being deciduous, bare-rooted trees are sold and planted while dormant in winter, the cheapest way to buy them; potted trees can go in through much of the year as long as you water them through summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Pruning: prune in summer, not winter
sections.body: This is the rule that catches people out: prune apricots in late summer or early autumn, after harvest, on a warm dry day, and never in winter. Apricots are very prone to bacterial canker and silver leaf, and a winter cut opens a fresh wound in cool, damp weather just when those infections are about, which is how a pruning cut ends up killing a whole limb. Pruning in warm, dry weather lets the wound seal quickly. Apricots fruit on short spurs that crop for several years and at the base of the previous summer's growth, so prune lightly to let light into an open vase shape and renew a little fruiting wood each year, rather than cutting hard.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water deeply through summer and right up to harvest, because apricots fill and sweeten in the last few weeks and a thirsty tree drops fruit and ripens it small, with no recovering the size later. Keep the water up after harvest too, while the tree sets the buds for next year. Apricots are only moderate feeders, so a single complete fruit-tree fertiliser in late winter is usually enough, leaning to potassium for fruiting rather than a high-nitrogen mix, since too much nitrogen pushes soft leafy growth at the expense of fruit and makes disease worse. Mulch well to hold soil moisture, and keep the mulch off the trunk.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Apricots open the stone-fruit season, ripening from about November to January across temperate Australia and earlier in warm districts. They gain very little sugar after picking, so leave them on the tree until they are fully coloured, slightly soft and fragrant, when the flavour and juice peak. Tree-ripe apricots bruise easily and keep only a few days in the fridge, which is exactly why so much of the crop is dried, bottled or made into jam. Pick over the tree every couple of days as the fruit comes on, since a heavy crop can ripen in a rush.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases to watch
sections.body: Brown rot is the main disease, rotting blossom in spring and fruit close to harvest in warm, wet weather, and it carries over on the shrivelled mummified fruit left on the tree, so clean these up. Apricots are also notably prone to bacterial canker and gummosis, which ooze gum from the bark and can kill a branch or a young tree outright, the very reason apricots are pruned in dry summer weather rather than in winter. Shot hole peppers the leaves with small holes and freckle spots the fruit in wet springs. One disease apricots largely escape is peach leaf curl, which troubles peaches and nectarines but not apricots. Fruit fly (Queensland fruit fly in the eastern states, Mediterranean fruit fly in Western Australia) is the key summer pest, so net or bait as the fruit colours.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than a seedling. Apricots do not come true from seed, so a seedling gives unpredictable fruit and takes years longer to bear, while grafted, named stock fruits to type. Named varieties are worked onto a rootstock, usually an apricot or Myrobalan plum seedling, with a low-chill peach rootstock used in warm districts, and dwarf forms suit a large pot or a small garden. Check the label for the variety name and its chill rating against your district before you buy. The in-stock table above shows the named apricots available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long until an apricot tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted, named apricot usually starts bearing in its second to fourth year and crops well by about year five. A tree grown from a swallowed stone takes much longer and the fruit is a gamble, which is why grafted, named stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Can I grow an apricot tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, if you choose a dwarf form such as a dwarf Trevatt, Storey's or Fireball. Stand it in full sun, use a free-draining mix, and water and feed regularly because containers dry out and lose nutrients fast. A potted tree has the bonus that you can move it to shelter the early blossom from frost and the ripening fruit from birds.

faqs.q: Do apricots get peach leaf curl like peaches do?
faqs.a: No. Peach leaf curl, the fungus that puckers and reddens the new leaves of peaches and nectarines, is not an apricot disease, so an apricot needs no spray for it. Apricots have their own troubles instead, mainly brown rot and shot hole, and if you do give a tree a late-winter copper spray it is those you are aiming at.

faqs.q: What is a plumcot, aprium or Plumscrumptious?
faqs.a: They are all crosses between a plum and an apricot. A plumcot or interspecific such as Plumscrumptious is a roughly even hybrid of the two, an aprium leans more to the apricot parent and a pluot leans more to the plum. They are grown just like an apricot, though some, including Plumscrumptious, need a compatible plum or apricot nearby to set fruit.

WA.intro: Growing apricots in Western Australia. The dry south-west spring and summer actually suit apricots, which hate humidity, but the mild winters on the Perth coastal plain give low chill, so variety choice and where you plant matter a great deal. WA's quarantine rules also shape where you can buy a tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west has a Mediterranean climate of hot dry summers and mild wet winters. The dry spring is a genuine advantage for apricots, because the brown rot and bacterial diseases that plague them in the humid eastern states get far less of a hold here. The catch is winter chill: the Perth coastal plain is mild and gives only low chill, so near the coast you lean on the low-chill apricots, while the cooler Perth Hills and the higher, colder inland districts carry enough chill for the richer heritage types.

sections.heading: Where apricots grow in WA
sections.body: Stone fruit is grown in three main WA districts: Gingin north of Perth, the Perth Hills, and the south-west around Donnybrook. The Perth Hills around Karragullen and Pickering Brook have long grown apricots, with the old Kalamunda orchards once packing thousands of cases a year, and Donnybrook is a long-established stone-fruit town. The commercial fruit belt runs in a broken line from Gingin south to Albany, with stone fruit concentrated in its northern, warmer half.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's warm, dry summers bring on the country's early apricots, picked from about November into January, ahead of the local peaches and plums. Leave the fruit to colour fully on the tree, because in the dry heat it ripens quickly once it starts.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly is established right across the south-west and apricots are among the fruit it attacks most, so baiting, netting and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit are essential. WA is free of Queensland fruit fly, the eastern-states pest, which is eradicated whenever it is detected here, so Medfly is the fly to plan around. The dry climate keeps brown rot and bacterial canker down compared with the east, though they can still appear in a wet spring. Rainbow lorikeets, a declared pest in WA, and other birds will strip a ripe apricot tree, so net as the fruit colours.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and are usually treated and certified before they can cross the border, and stone-fruit (Prunus) nursery stock is tightly controlled. Many eastern nurseries will not post apricot trees to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Which apricot varieties suit Perth and the coastal plain?
faqs.a: Near the warm coast choose low-chill varieties such as Newcastle, Glengarry or a low-chill Fireball, which fruit on mild winters. Up in the cooler Perth Hills and the colder inland you can also grow the higher-chill heritage apricots like Moorpark, Trevatt and Storey's, which repay the extra cold with richer fruit.

faqs.q: Can apricots cope with Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, the sharp drainage of Perth sand actually suits apricots, which hate wet feet, better than it suits many fruit trees. Dig in compost, mulch well to hold moisture over the shallow roots, use a soil wetting agent if the sand has turned water-repellent, and feed a little and often because nutrients leach quickly through sand.

QLD.intro: Growing apricots in Queensland. Apricots are a poor fit for most of the state: they need a cold winter and dry weather, so in Queensland they are really only a crop for the high, cold Granite Belt, not the humid coast or the subtropics.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Apricots need a proper winter chill to flower and dislike warm, humid conditions, which bring on brown rot, bacterial disease and dieback. That rules out almost all of Queensland: the subtropical south-east, the coast around Brisbane and the wet tropics are too warm, too humid and too low in chill for apricots to crop reliably. Treated honestly, apricot is a cold-district crop here and not a coastal one.

sections.heading: Where apricots grow in QLD
sections.body: The one part of Queensland reliably cold enough is the Granite Belt around Stanthorpe and Ballandean, in the state's far south near the New South Wales border. Sitting around 800 metres and higher, it is the coldest part of Queensland, with real winter chill and hard frosts, and it is the state's traditional deciduous stone-fruit district. Production falls away quickly as you drop toward the warmer coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: On the Granite Belt apricots ripen in early summer, with the district's stone fruit coming on in volume from about mid-November and through December. They open the season ahead of the warm-climate peaches and nectarines grown closer to the coast.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic across the state and is the major pest, stinging ripening apricots, so trapping, baiting and netting are part of growing here. Queensland's humidity also drives brown rot and bacterial spot harder than a dry inland climate would, so an open tree, good airflow and orchard hygiene all help.

faqs.q: Can you grow apricots in Brisbane or on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Realistically, no. The coast and the subtropical south-east are too humid and too mild in winter for apricots to flower and ripen well, and the damp brings constant disease pressure. If you want a stone fruit there, a low-chill peach, nectarine or plum is a far surer bet than an apricot.

faqs.q: Are apricots harder to grow in Queensland than peaches or plums?
faqs.a: Yes. Breeders have produced good low-chill peaches, nectarines and plums (the Florida-bred Gulf and Flordaprince types) that fruit through a warm winter, but there is no equivalent widely grown low-chill apricot. So apricots stay confined to the cold Granite Belt while those other stone fruits can be grown much more widely across the state.

NSW.intro: Growing apricots in New South Wales. The state runs from a warm, humid coast to cold inland tablelands, and apricots want the cold: the cool Central West and the high South West Slopes grow them well, while the warm coast is marginal.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Apricots need solid winter chill and dry weather, so in New South Wales they belong inland rather than on the warm, humid coast. The cool Central Tablelands and the high country around Young carry plenty of chill, but they bring their own risk: apricots flower earliest of the stone fruit, so a late spring frost can catch the blossom in these cold districts. The warm, humid Sydney basin and the coast are low in chill and harder on disease, which makes them marginal for apricots.

sections.heading: Where apricots grow in NSW
sections.body: The cool Central West around Orange, Bathurst, Cowra and Mudgee grows apricots and other stone fruit, and the Hilltops district around Young, high on the South West Slopes above 500 metres, is well known for its cherries and stone fruit. Further west, the irrigated Riverina around Griffith and Leeton grows apricots commercially along the Murrumbidgee. Each of these districts has the cool winters apricots need, away from the humid coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The warmer, irrigated inland picks first, with apricots in the Young district from about November into December, while the cooler, higher Central West around Orange ripens later, into December and January. The state's spread of districts gives a long apricot season overall.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established across most of New South Wales and is the main pest of apricots, so trap and bait from late spring and clean up any fallen fruit. Brown rot rots blossom and fruit in warm, wet spells near harvest, and apricots are prone to bacterial canker and gummosis, which kill branches, so keep all pruning to dry summer weather rather than winter.

faqs.q: How do I protect apricot blossom from frost on the NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: Because apricots flower so early, their blossom is the most frost-exposed of all the stone fruit. Plant on a slope or rise where cold air drains away rather than in a low frost hollow, keep a young tree small enough to throw a cover over on a frosty night, and lean toward a slightly later-flowering variety if your district is very cold.

faqs.q: Which apricots suit the warm Sydney coast?
faqs.a: The coast is marginal, but a low-chill variety such as Glengarry or Newcastle has the best chance. Give it the sunniest, most open spot you have, prune to keep the centre airy, and stay on top of brown rot, because the coastal humidity is the real enemy. Many gardeners near the coast get more reliable fruit from a low-chill peach or plum.

VIC.intro: Growing apricots in Victoria. Victoria grows more apricots than any other state, helped by cold winters that reliably supply the chill apricots need and warm, dry inland summers that ripen first-class fruit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cool temperate winters easily meet the chill apricots want, and the warm, dry summers of the inland ripen them beautifully, which is why the state grows the bulk of Australia's apricots. The main weather risk is frost: apricots flower very early, in late winter, so a hard spring frost can cut the crop, and growers in the fruit districts watch the forecast and use frost protection when it threatens.

sections.heading: Where apricots grow in VIC
sections.body: The Goulburn Valley around Shepparton, Cobram, Kyabram, Tatura and Ardmona is Australia's stone-fruit and fruit-canning heartland, and apricots are one of its mainstay crops. The hot, irrigated Sunraysia around Mildura and Swan Hill grows early apricots along the Murray, while cooler districts such as the Yarra Valley ripen their fruit later.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Apricots are very early maturing, so they open Victoria's stone-fruit season: the hot Sunraysia district picks first, from about late November, the Goulburn Valley main crop runs through December into January, and the coolest districts finish last.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is now established across northern Victoria, including the Goulburn Murray Valley and the Sunraysia and Swan Hill districts, so Victoria can no longer be treated as fruit-fly-free and growers there trap and bait as a matter of course; the old Greater Sunraysia Pest Free Area was wound up in 2024. Brown rot is the key disease, worst in wet weather near harvest, and spring frost can damage the early blossom.

faqs.q: Is Victoria still free of fruit fly?
faqs.a: No, not any more. The pest has spread into the fruit-growing north of the state, so even a backyard apricot now needs the same care as one across the border in New South Wales: net the tree or bait for the fly, and clear up any windfalls. The old idea of Victoria as a fly-free place to grow fruit is well out of date.

faqs.q: Why is Victoria so good for apricots?
faqs.a: Two things line up: cold winters that reliably deliver the chill apricots need, and hot, dry inland summers that ripen rich, sweet fruit. That pairing is why the state grows more apricots than anywhere else in Australia. Shelter the early blossom from a spring frost and a Victorian apricot will crop heavily year after year.
